mnemonics.title: File Operations Order
mnemonics.content: COORDS: Create → Open → Read/Write → Delete → Seek - Remember: Always OPEN before READ/WRITE, CLOSE when done!

mnemonics.title: File Descriptor Numbers
mnemonics.content: 0 = stdin, 1 = stdout, 2 = stderr - First three are standard! Everything else starts at 3.

mnemonics.title: File Access Methods
mnemonics.content: SAS: Sequential Access Sorts records; DAS: Direct Access Seeks anywhere - SAS uses read-next, DAS uses seek-then-read.

mnemonics.title: File Attributes Remember
mnemonics.content: NATO: Name, Attributes (permissions), Type, Owner, Times (created/modified/accessed), Size, Location (disk address)

acronyms.expansion: Open Read Only - Read access only, no modifications allowed
acronyms.usage: Used when file is to be only read, ensures no accidental writes

acronyms.expansion: Open Write Only - Write access only, cannot read back
acronyms.usage: Used for output files where you only need to write data

acronyms.expansion: Open Read-Write - Both read and write operations allowed
acronyms.usage: Used for files that need both reading and modification

acronyms.expansion: Open Create - Create file if it doesn't exist
acronyms.usage: Ensures the file exists before opening, prevents errors

acronyms.expansion: Open Append - Seek to end before each write
acronyms.usage: Used for log files where data is always added at the end

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two tables: Per-Process (small, holds descriptor# → pointer) and System-wide (large, holds offset, mode, inode pointer). Descriptor is just an INDEX into per-process table.
Think of a cursor on a tape measure. Initially at 0. Read moves right by bytes read; Write writes at cursor then moves right. Seek jumps to specific position.
Imagine a flag that automatically runs 'seek to end' BEFORE every write operation. Not at open time, but each write! This is atomic.

analogies.analogy: Like a library card catalog number - you don't handle the book directly, you give the number and the librarian (OS) finds and manages the book for you.
Like a shopping cart at a store - you collect several items (data) then make one trip to checkout (disk I/O) instead of walking to the register for each item.
Like your finger marking your place in a book - tells you where the next page turn (read/write) will start. Moving your finger = seek().
Shared lock = multiple people reading a whiteboard (no writing). Exclusive lock = one person erasing and rewriting the entire whiteboard (no one else can even look).
